1.1: ). Now, the guards are the arms and hands. ― Those who , etc.; these refer to the teeth which, in old people, are usually few in number and can no longer chew or bite food, and are therefore idle. Those who watch ; That is to say, the eyes. Through the windows ; through the orbits, the cavities in the skull where the eyes are located.
1.14: . This is the dominant thought of Ecclesiastes and the explanation of the book.
1.4: apparently the lips that old people close when eating, being forced to clench their jaws and gums to chew, a defect in their teeth. the millstone , can be heard from the mouth itself. Compare to the preceding verse. ― That we will get up , etc. The sleep of old people is short and often interrupted; the slightest song of a small bird wakes them. The singing girls , These are properly the organs of the voice, such as the lungs, the epiglottis, the teeth, the lips, etc., as well as the ears, which, in old age, no longer hear and, consequently, are entirely insensitive to any kind of song and harmony. Scripture offers us an example of this in the person of the octogenarian Berzelai (see
1.8: ); the experience of Solomon, the wisest of men, who tried everything, proves this (verses
1.12: , and its final fate, see Ecclesiastes ,
1.18: If the wise man advises, as we have just seen, against excess in justice and wisdom, all the more so will he make the same recommendation when it comes to evil and impiety. Thus, in forbidding excessive impiety, he does not thereby permit a little impiety; he means only that since human life cannot be without flaws and sin, one must at least avoid great disorder, frequent falls, and bad habits.
1.26: . We must be charitable, even if we make some ungrateful, for those to whom we do good may, after all, be grateful, chapter
1.13: , on which his happiness depends, see Ecclesiastes ,
1.17: ); the good, like the wicked, are subject to Providence, whose will is inscrutable (chapter
1.5: We will also fear , etc. The elderly will be afraid to climb, because their legs will no longer be flexible, nor their chests and breathing free and unobstructed; and even on the path itself, they will walk with difficulty, always fearing they will stumble, encountering a rough and uneven trail. The almond tree blossoms ; That is to say, the hair of old men will appear like the almond blossom, which blooms white. The grasshopper is becoming heavy. ; That is to say, it will become heavy and cumbersome, so that it will no longer be able to fly or jump; another image of man who has reached old age. The caper no longer has any effect. . This shrub, opening slightly, produces a white flower that soon falls, revealing a kind of oblong acorn. Now, this flower quite naturally represents the white
1.6: The silver cord And the golden bulb These probably represent the bonds that tie us to life. The jug Who breaks on the fountain can mean the heart breaking at the source of life, and the pulley that breaks , the lungs that no longer draw in air.
1.7: This verse is enough to avenge the author of Ecclesiastes from the reproach of materialism; it is impossible to express oneself more clearly on the dogma of the survival of the soul to the body.
1.24: The author's aim in this verse is to warn us against sordid avarice and the passion for seeking riches, saying that it is better to spend one's life enjoying the fruits of one's labors in moderation, as gifts from the Creator, than to deprive oneself of them in order to be consumed by immoderate worries and a vain pursuit of the false goods of this world. Thus, there is no proof that this author is an Epicurean, as some unbelievers would have it.
1.3: The house guards , etc. The human body is compared here to a house, just as in Job ,
1.21: There is no more materialism in this verse than in the preceding ones. Solomon simply states, and this is undeniable, that human reason cannot clearly discern, by its own strength alone, what fate awaits man after his death.
1.31: . ― Oppressors ; literally two , that is to say, those who are the authors of oppressions and tears which has just been mentioned.
1.15: The fool is so lazy that all work tires and overwhelms him; he is at the same time so ignorant and stupid that he doesn't even know the way to town.
1.16: The 4th e The final section of the book of Ecclesiastes begins here in verse
1.10: ). The sacred books, which teach us true wisdom, lead to true happiness (verses
1.35: ). We will observe, on this occasion, that in Hebrew the expression son Or daughter of a thing gives itself to everything that depends on this thing, to everything that belongs to it, to everything that has a connection with it. ― The image taken from the The faint voice of the one who grinds is very natural in Palestine. The sound of the millstone grinding grain characterized inhabited places in the East, just as the noise of cars characterizes the large cities of the West. — The figures and metaphors used in this description of old age may seem rather elaborate to us, but they are entirely in keeping with the taste of Orientals. At the end of a Syriac manuscript of Holy Scripture, the scribe wrote this prayer: «Deign, Lord, not to deprive us of the reward of the five twin sisters who toi
1.37: times in twelve chapters; this is indeed the fear God which is man's duty, see Ecclesiastes ,

2.1: I said in my heart, "Come then, I will test you with joy, taste pleasure." And behold, this too is vanity.
2.2: I said of laughter: "Nonsense" and of joy: "What does it produce?"«
2.3: I applied myself in my heart to surrender my flesh to wine, while my heart would lead me with wisdom and cling to folly, until I saw what is good for the children of men to do under heaven during the days of their lives.
2.4: I undertook great projects, I built houses for myself, I planted vineyards,
2.5: I made myself gardens and orchards and planted all kinds of fruit trees there,
2.6: I made myself water reservoirs to water the groves where the trees grew.
2.7: I bought male and female servants and had their children born in the house; I also had herds of cattle and sheep, more than all who were before me in Jerusalem.
2.8: I also amassed silver and gold and the riches of kings and provinces, I procured male and female singers and the delights of the children of men, women in abundance.
2.9: I grew great and surpassed all who were before me in Jerusalem, and even my wisdom remained with me.
2.10: Whatever my eyes desired, I did not deny them; I did not withhold any joy from my heart, for my heart took pleasure in all my labor, and this was my share of all my labor.
2.11: Then I considered all my works that my hands had done and the labor that they had cost me, and behold, all is vanity and a striving after wind, and there is no profit under the sun.
2.12: So I turned my gaze to wisdom to compare it with folly and madness. For who is the man who can come after the king, to whom this dignity was conferred long ago?
2.13: And I saw that wisdom has as much advantage over folly as light has over darkness:
2.14: The wise man has his eyes where they should be, and the fool walks in darkness. And I also recognized that the same fate will befall them both.
2.15: And I said in my heart, "The same fate as that of the fool will befall me also; what then is the use of all my wisdom?" And I said in my heart, "That too is vanity.".
2.16: For the memory of the wise is no more eternal than that of the fool; in the days that follow, both are equally forgotten. Indeed, the wise die just as easily as the fool.
2.17: And I hated life, because what is done under the sun is evil in my eyes, for all is vanity and a chasing after the wind.
2.18: And I hated all my work, which I did under the sun, and which I will leave to the man who will come after me.
2.19: And who knows whether he will be wise or foolish? Yet he will be master of my work, in which I have labored and used my wisdom under the sun. This too is vanity.
2.20: And I have come to give my heart over to discouragement, because of all the work I have done under the sun.
2.21: For if a man who has displayed wisdom, intelligence, and skill in his work, leaves the fruit of his labor to a man who has not worked at it: this too is vanity and a great evil.
2.22: For what does man gain from all his toil and his anxious thoughts, which weary him under the sun?
2.23: All her days are filled with pain, her occupations with sorrows, even at night her heart does not rest: this too is vanity.
2.24: There is nothing better for a man than to eat and drink and to find satisfaction in his work, but I have seen that this too comes from the hand of God.
2.25: Indeed, who can eat and enjoy well-being without it?
2.26: For to the one who is good in his sight, he gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy, but to the sinner, he gives the task of gathering and hoarding, to give to the one who is good in God's sight. This too is vanity and a chasing after the wind.

3.1: There is an appointed time for everything, and a season for every activity under heaven:
3.2: A time to be born and a time to die, a time to plant and a time to uproot what has been planted,
3.3: a time to kill and a time to heal, a time to tear down and a time to build,
3.4: A time to cry and a time to laugh, a time to mourn and a time to dance,
3.5: a time to throw stones and a time to pick them up, a time to embrace and a time to refrain from embracing.
3.6: A time to search and a time to lose, a time to keep and a time to throw away,
3.7: A time to tear and a time to mend, a time to be silent and a time to speak,
3.8: A time to love and a time to hate, a time for war and a time for peace.
3.9: What is the advantage, for the one who works, of the effort he puts in?
3.10: I have examined the labor which God requires of the children of men:
3.11: God has made everything beautiful in its time, and has also set eternity in their hearts; yet no one can fathom what God has done from beginning to end.
3.12: And I recognized that there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and give themselves well-being during their lives,
3.13: and at the same time that if a man eats and drinks and enjoys well-being in the midst of his work, this is a gift from God.
3.14: I have recognized that everything God does will endure forever; nothing can be added to it and nothing taken from it. God does this so that we may fear him.
3.15: What is being done already existed and what will be done has already been: God brings back what has passed.
3.16: I have seen something else under the sun: that in the very seat of justice there is wickedness, and in the place of righteousness there is iniquity.
3.17: I said in my heart, "God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every activity and for every work."«
3.18: I said in my heart concerning the children of men, «This is how it is done, so that God may test them, and that they may see that they are in themselves like the beasts.»
3.19: For the fate of the children of men is the fate of the beast: they have the same fate, as one dies, the other dies also; there is only one breath for all; man has no advantage over the beast, for all is vanity.
3.20: Everything goes to one place, everything comes from dust and everything returns to dust.
3.21: Who knows the breath of the children of men, which ascends upward, and the breath of the beast, which descends downward to the earth?
3.22: And I saw that there is nothing better for a person than to rejoice in their work: this is their lot. For who will enable them to know what will happen after them?

4.1: I turned and saw all the oppressions that are committed under the sun, and behold, the oppressed are in tears and there is no one to comfort them. They are subjected to the violence of their oppressors and there is no one to comfort them.
4.2: And I proclaimed the dead who are already dead happier than the living who are still alive,
4.3: And happier than both is he who has not yet come into existence, who has not seen the evil deeds that are committed under the sun.
4.4: I have seen that all labor and all skill in a work is but envy against a man from his neighbor: this too is vanity and a chasing after the wind.
4.5: The fool folds his hands and eats his own flesh.
4.6: Better is one hand full of rest than two full of toil and chasing the wind
4.7: I turned around and saw another vanity under the sun.
4.8: Such a man is alone and has no second, he has neither son nor brother, and yet there is no end to all his labor and his eyes are never sated with riches: "For whom then do I labor and deprive my soul of enjoyment?" This too is vanity and a bad occupation.
4.9: It's better to live as a couple than alone; both partners receive a good salary from their work.,
4.10: For if they fall, one can help his companion up. But woe to him who is alone and falls without having a second to help him up.
4.11: Similarly, if two people sleep together, they keep each other warm, but how could a man alone get warm?
4.12: And if someone controls the one who is alone, both will be able to resist him, and the tripled thread does not break easily.
4.13: Better is a poor but wise young man than an old and foolish king who no longer knows how to listen to advice.,
4.14: because he comes out of prison to reign, although he was born poor in his kingdom.
4.15: I saw all the living walking under the sun near the young man who was rising in place of the old king.
4.16: There was no end to that multitude, to all those he led. And yet his descendants will not rejoice over him. That too is vanity and a chasing after the wind.
4.17: Take care of your foot when you go to the house of God; to approach and listen is better than to offer sacrifices like fools, for their ignorance leads them to do harm.

5.1: Do not be hasty in opening your mouth, nor let your heart be quick to utter anything before God, for God is in heaven and you are on earth; therefore let your words be few.
5.2: For out of a multitude of occupations come dreams, and out of a multitude of words, foolish talk.
5.3: When you make a vow to God, do not delay in fulfilling it, for there is no favor for fools: what you vow, fulfill it.
5.4: It is better for you not to vow, than to vow and not fulfill.
5.5: Do not let your mouth cause your flesh to sin, and do not say in the presence of the messenger of God that it was an oversight: why should God be angry about your words and destroy the work of your hands?
5.6: For as there is vanity in many pursuits, so there is also vanity in many words; therefore fear God.
5.7: If you see in a province the poor oppressed, and law and justice violated, do not be surprised at the thing, for a greater one watches over a greater one, and even greater ones watch over them.
5.8: An advantage for the country in every respect is a king who takes care of agriculture.
5.9: He who loves money will not be satisfied with money, and he who loves riches will not taste their fruit; this too is vanity.
5.10: When goods multiply, those who consume them also multiply, and what advantage does it bring to their owners except that they see them with their eyes?
5.11: The sleep of the worker is sweet, whether he has little or much to eat, but the satiety of the rich does not let him sleep.
5.12: There is a grave evil that I have seen under the sun: riches hoarded to the harm of the one who possesses them:
5.13: These riches are lost through some unfortunate event and, if he has fathered a son, he has nothing left in his hands.
5.14: Just as he came from his mother's womb, so he shall return naked, as he came, and he shall receive nothing for his work, that he may carry it in his hand:
5.15: This is yet another serious evil, that he should leave as he came: and what advantage does he gain from having worked for the wind?
5.16: Moreover, all his life he eats in darkness, he has much sorrow, suffering and irritation.
5.17: So this is what I have seen: it is good and pleasant for a man to eat and drink and to find satisfaction in all his toil, which he undertakes under the sun, during the days of his life which God gives him, for this is his lot.
5.18: Moreover, to everyone to whom God gives riches and possessions, with the power to eat them, to take his share and to rejoice in his work, this is a gift from God.
5.19: For then he hardly thinks about the days of his life, because God fills his heart with joy.

6.1: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, and this evil is great for mankind:
6.2: Such a man, to whom God has given riches, treasures, and honor, and who lacks nothing for his soul that he could desire, but God does not allow him to enjoy it, for a stranger enjoys it: this is vanity and a grievous evil.
6.3: If a man had fathered a hundred sons, lived for many years, and the days of his years had multiplied, if his soul was not satiated with happiness and he did not even have a burial, I say that a stillborn child is happier than he.
6.4: For he came in vain, he departs into darkness, and darkness will cover his name.,
6.5: He has neither seen nor known the sun, yet he has more rest than this man.
6.6: And even if he were to live two thousand years, without enjoying happiness, wouldn't everything still go to the same place?
6.7: All of man's work is for his mouth, but his desires are never satisfied.
6.8: For what advantage does the wise have over the fool? What advantage does the poor have who knows how to conduct themselves before the living?
6.9: What the eyes see is preferable to the wandering of desires. That too is vanity and chasing after the wind.
6.10: Of everything that happens, the name has already been spoken; we know what a man will be, and he cannot argue with anyone stronger than himself.
6.11: For there are many words that only increase vanity: what advantage does it bring to man?
6.12: For who knows what is good for a person in life, during the fleeting days of their life, which pass away like a shadow? And who can tell a person what will happen under the sun after them?

7.1: A good reputation is better than good perfume, and the day of death better than the day of birth.
7.2: It is better to go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting, for in the former appears the end of every man, and the living apply their heart to it.
7.3: Sadness is better than laughter, for a sad face is good for the heart.
7.4: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, and the heart of fools is in the house of joy.
7.5: It is better to heed the rebuke of the wise than to hear the song of fools.
7.6: For like the crackling of thorns under the boiler is the laughter of fools: that too is vanity.
7.7: For oppression makes the wise foolish, and gifts corrupt the heart.
7.8: Better is the end of a thing than its beginning, better is a patient mind than a haughty one.
7.9: Do not be quick to become angry in your mind, for anger resides in the bosom of fools.
7.10: Do not say, «Why were the former days better than these?» For it is not from wisdom that you ask about this.
7.11: Wisdom is good with an inheritance and profitable to those who see the sun.
7.12: For like the protection of money, like the protection of wisdom, but one advantage of knowledge is that wisdom gives life to those who possess it.
7.13: Look at the work of God: who can straighten what he has bent?
7.14: In the day of happiness, be joyful, and in the day of adversity, consider: God has made the one as well as the other, so that man may not discover what is going to happen to him.
7.15: All this I saw in the day of my vanity: there is a righteous man who perishes in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man who prolongs his life in his wickedness.
7.16: Don't be excessively righteous, and don't be excessively wise: why would you want to destroy yourself?
7.17: Do not be excessively wicked and do not be foolish: why would you want to die before your time?
7.18: It is good that you remember this and do not let go of that, for he who fears God avoids all these excesses.
7.19: Wisdom gives the wise person more strength than ten rulers in the city.
7.20: For there is no righteous man on earth who does good without ever sinning.
7.21: Do not pay attention to all the words that are spoken, lest you hear your servant cursing you.,
7.22: because your heart knows that you too have cursed others many times.
7.23: I recognized all this as true through wisdom, I said: I want to be wise, but wisdom remained far from me.
7.24: What is coming is distant, deep, deep: who can reach it?
7.25: I applied myself and my heart sought to know, to fathom and to pursue the wisdom and reason of things, and I recognized that wickedness is madness and that foolish conduct is delusion.
7.26: And I found more bitter than death the woman whose heart is a snare and a net, and whose hands are fetters; he who pleases God escapes her, but the sinner will be entangled by her.
7.27: “Look,” says Ecclesiastes, “I have found this, considering things one by one to discover the reason, which my soul has constantly sought, without finding it: I have found one man among a thousand, but I have not found one woman among the same number.”.
7.28: But look, I have found this: God made man upright, but they seek many subtleties.

8.1: Who is like the wise man, and who knows the explanation of things like him? A man's wisdom makes his face shine, and the roughness of his countenance is transfigured.
8.2: I tell you: Observe the king's orders, because of the oath you swore to God.,
8.3: Do not hasten to distance yourself from him. Do not persist in evil, for whatever he desires, he can do.,
8.4: The king's word, in fact, is sovereign, and who will say to him, "What are you doing?"«
8.5: He who observes the precept experiences no harm, and the heart of the wise will know the time and the judgment.
8.6: For everything there is a time and a judgment, for great is the evil that will come upon man.
8.7: He doesn't know what will happen, and who will tell him how it will happen?
8.8: Man is not master of his breath, nor can he hold his breath, and he has no power over the day of his death; there is no exemption in this fight, and crime cannot save a man.
8.9: I have seen all these things, applying my heart to all the work that is done under the sun, in a time when one man rules over another to the latter's harm.
8.10: And then I saw the wicked receive burial and enter their rest, while men who acted uprightly go far from the holy place and are forgotten in the city; this too is vanity.
8.11: Because the sentence pronounced against evil deeds is not carried out hastily, for this reason the hearts of the children of men are emboldened within them to do evil,
8.12: But although a sinner does evil a hundred times and prolongs his days, I know that it will be well with those who fear God, who are in fear before him.
8.13: But happiness is not for the wicked, and like a shadow, he will not prolong his days, because he does not fear God.
8.14: There is another vanity that occurs on earth: There are righteous people to whom things happen that are fitting for the deeds of the wicked, and there are wicked people to whom things happen that are fitting for the deeds of the righteous. I say that this too is vanity.
8.15: Therefore I praised joy, because there is nothing better for a man under the sun than to eat and drink and be glad, and this is what should accompany him in his work, during the days of life that God gives him under the sun.
8.16: When I applied my heart to know wisdom and to consider the task that is being accomplished on earth, for neither by day nor by night does man see sleep with his eyes,
8.17: I have seen all the work of God, I have seen that man cannot find the work that is done under the sun, man tires himself out searching and does not find, even if the wise man wants to know, he cannot find.

9.1: Indeed, I have taken all this to heart and observed all this: That the righteous and the wise and their works are in the hand of God, man knows neither love nor hate: all is before them.
9.2: Everything happens equally to everyone: the same fate befalls the righteous and the wicked, the good and pure and the impure, those who sacrifice and those who do not. As it happens to the good, so it happens to the sinner; it is the same for those who swear an oath as for those who fear to swear an oath.
9.3: It is an evil among all that is done under the sun, that there should be one fate for all; therefore the heart of the sons of men is full of malice and madness is in their hearts while they live, after which they go to the dead.
9.4: For the man who is among the living, there is hope; a living dog is better than a dead lion.
9.5: The living, indeed, know that they will die, but the dead know nothing and there is no more reward for them, for their memory is forgotten.
9.6: Their love, their hate, their envy have already perished, and they will never again have any part in what is done under the sun.
9.7: Go, eat your bread with joy and drink your wine with a glad heart, since God already shows favor to your works.
9.8: May your clothes always be white and may perfumed oil never be lacking on your head.
9.9: Enjoy life with a wife whom you love, during all the days of your vain life that God has given you under the sun, during all your vain days, for this is your portion in life and in the work that you do under the sun.
9.10: Whatever your hand can do, do it with your strength, for there is no more working, nor understanding, nor knowledge, nor wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going.
9.11: I turned and saw under the sun that the race is not to the swift, nor the war to the valiant, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to the learned; for time and accidents overtake them all.
9.12: For man does not even know his hour, like fish that are caught in a fatal net, like birds that are caught in a snare, like them the children of men are entangled in the time of misfortune, when it suddenly falls upon them.
9.13: I have once again seen this trait of wisdom under the sun, and it seemed to me great.
9.14: There was a small town, with few men within its walls, a powerful king came against it, besieged it and built high towers against it.
9.15: And there was found a poor but wise man, who saved the city by his wisdom. And no one remembered that poor man.
9.16: And I said, «Wisdom is better than strength, but the wisdom of the poor is despised, and their words are not heeded.»
9.17: The words of the wise, spoken calmly, are listened to better than the shouts of a ruler among fools.
9.18: Wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one sinner can destroy much good.

10.1: Dead flies infect and corrupt the perfumer's oil, just as a little madness triumphs over wisdom and glory.
10.2: The heart of the wise is at his right hand, and the heart of the fool at his left.
10.3: And also, when the fool goes down the path, he lacks meaning and shows everyone that he is crazy.
10.4: If the prince's spirit rises against you, do not leave your place, for calmness prevents great mistakes.
10.5: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, like an error that comes from the sovereign:
10.6: Madness occupies the high positions and the rich sit in low conditions.
10.7: I saw slaves carried on horses and princes walking like slaves.
10.8: He who digs a pit may fall into it, and he who knocks down a wall may be bitten by a snake.
10.9: He who dislodges stones may be injured, and he who splits wood may hurt himself.
10.10: If the iron is dull and the edge has not been sharpened, one will have to redouble one's strength, but wisdom is preferable for success.
10.11: If the snake bites due to lack of enchantment, there is no advantage for the enchanter.
10.12: The words of the wise man's mouth are full of grace, but the lips of the fool devour him.
10.13: The beginning of the words from his mouth is foolishness, and the end of his speech is raging madness.
10.14: And the fool multiplies words. Man does not know what will happen, and who can tell him what will happen after him?
10.15: The work of the fool tires him, he who does not even know how to go to the city.
10.16: Woe to you, land whose king is a child and whose princes eat from the morning.
10.17: Happy are you, land whose king is the son of nobles and whose princes eat at the proper time, to sustain their strength and not to indulge in drink.
10.18: When hands are lazy, the framework sags, and when hands are loose, the house leaks.
10.19: We eat meals to enjoy the pleasure, wine makes life joyful, and money answers everything.
10.20: Even in your thoughts do not curse the king, even in your bedroom do not curse the powerful, for the bird of the sky would carry away your voice and the winged beast would proclaim your words.

11.1: Cast your bread upon the face of the waters, for after many days you will find it again.,
11.2: Give a portion to seven or even to eight, for you do not know what disaster may occur on earth.
11.3: When the clouds are filled with rain, they empty themselves onto the earth, and if a tree falls in the south or the north, it remains in the place where it fell.
11.4: He who observes the wind will not sow, and he who questions the clouds will not reap.
11.5: Just as you do not know the way of the wind, or how bones are formed in the womb, so you do not know the works of God, who makes all things.
11.6: Sow your seed in the morning and do not let your hand rest in the evening, for you do not know which will succeed, this or that, or whether both are equally good.
11.7: The light is soft and it is a pleasure for the eye to see the sun.
11.8: Even if a man lives many years, let him rejoice during all those years and let him remember the days of darkness, for they will be many: all that happens is vanity.
11.9: Young man, rejoice in your youth, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your youth. Walk in the ways of your heart and in the sight of your eyes, but know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.
11.10: Banish sorrow from your heart and remove evil from your flesh; youth and adolescence are vanity.

12.1: And remember your Creator in the days of your youth, before the evil days come and the years approach of which you will say, «I have no pleasure in them.»
12.2: before the sun and the light and the moon and the stars darken, and the clouds return after the rain,
12.3: on the day when the guardians of the house tremble, when the strong men bow down, when those who grind stop because their numbers are diminished, when those who look out of the windows grow dark,
12.4: where the two leaves of the door close on the street, while the noise of the millstone fades, where one rises at the song of the bird, where all the daughters of song disappear,
12.5: where high places are feared, where terrors reign on the path, where the almond tree blossoms, where the grasshopper becomes a nuisance and the caper loses its power, for man departs for his eternal home and mourners roam the streets,
12.6: before the silver cord breaks, before the golden bulb shatters, before the pitcher breaks at the fountain, before the pulley breaks and rolls in the cistern,
12.7: and let the dust return to the earth, as it was, and let the spirit return to God who gave it.
12.8: Vanity of vanities, says Ecclesiastes, all is vanity.
12.9: Besides being a wise man, Ecclesiastes also taught knowledge to the people; he weighed and searched, and he set forth a great number of proverbs.
12.10: Ecclesiastes strove to find pleasing language and to write accurately words of truth.
12.11: The words of the wise are like goads and their collections like nails driven in; they are given by one Shepherd.
12.12: And as for more words than these, my son, be warned: multiplying books would have no end, and much study is a weariness to the flesh.
12.13: The speech ended, and everything was heard: Fear God and observe his commandments, for this is the whole of man.
12.14: For God will bring to account every hidden thing, every work, whether good or evil.
